title: OAuth callback does not validate a `state` parameter before exchanging the authorization code
reasoning: The route accepts any incoming `code` query parameter and immediately exchanges it for tokens, but it never checks a caller-bound `state` value. In an OAuth redirect handler, that omission allows CSRF/account-mixup flows where a victim can be driven through this callback with an attacker-controlled authorization result, causing the app to capture the wrong Spotify account's refresh token.
reproduction: Start an OAuth flow against Spotify using a different Spotify account, then send the resulting `/api/spotify/callback?code=...` URL to a user who is supposed to connect their own account. This handler will exchange the attacker-generated code because it has no state correlation.
recommendation: Generate a per-flow `state` token when starting Spotify auth, store it server-side or in a signed cookie, require the callback to include the same value, and reject mismatches before calling Spotify. If this route is used in production, prefer PKCE as well.
whyTestsDoNotAlreadyCoverThis: No callback tests were included, and the implementation has no assertions around expected `state` handling.
suggestedRegressionTest: Add a route test that calls `GET /api/spotify/callback` with a valid-looking `code` but missing or mismatched `state` and asserts that the route returns 400 without issuing the token exchange fetch.
